explicit_text: I left Japan in March of 1914 to get married. I met Mr. Iijima at a church in Matsuo before coming to America. Although I planned to return to Japan in about three years, I never managed to go back. My husband was in Japan during the war and wrote to me urging me to return, but I stayed in the U.S. for my son's sake.
implicit_text: I left my home country in March of 1914 to get married. I met Mr. Iijima at a church in a northern town before coming to America. explicit_text: I first met Mr. Iijima at a church in Matsuo, Iwate before leaving Japan. It was there that our relationship began, although he later returned to Japan. My older brother eventually agreed to my marriage after his parents came from far away to ask me to become his son's bride.

explicit_text: I had some quarrels with my sister who was in Hawaii when I was preparing to come to America. She was part of my family connections, but for some reason I could not come to America immediately. Hawaii was mentioned as a place where my sister lived during that time.

explicit_text: Before leaving Japan, I belonged to a Christian church in Azabu. This was part of my religious background before coming to America. Being a Christian was important to me, and I continued attending church after arriving in the U.S.

explicit_text: When I first came to America, I arrived in San Francisco. I was baptized there around April 30 after encouragement from a missionary and my brother. My husband had a small milk delivery business there, and later he tried to start a candy company like Morinaga but it did not succeed. I also went to beauty school in San Francisco to learn the Marinaro system.

I also went to beauty school in that city to learn the Marinaro system.

explicit_text: My brother was studying at a theological seminary in San Anselmo, California. He encouraged me to be baptized and wanted me to learn English. He also came to see me on his way to a church in Seattle during a difficult time in my marriage.

explicit_text: Rev. Watanabe's wife was teaching English at the YWCA, and my brother asked her to teach me English. However, I was not able to attend classes there. Some people learned English at the YWCA before joining their husbands, but I did not have that freedom.

explicit_text: My child's daughter graduated from Aoyama Gakuin University in Japan. She later came to college here in the U.S. Her husband's father is a minister, which connects our family to the church community. This shows the continuation of education and faith in our family.

explicit_text: My husband planned to start a candy company similar to Morinaga & Company in Japan. He learned candy making from a candy maker but unfortunately, the business did not succeed as he had hoped. This was during our time in San Francisco before moving to Sacramento.
implicit_text: My husband planned to start a candy company similar to a well-known Japanese confectioner. He learned candy making from a candy maker but unfortunately, the business did not succeed as he had hoped. This was during our time in San Francisco before moving to Sacramento.

explicit_text: We moved to Sacramento because the climate in San Francisco was not good for my health. I opened a beauty shop there after buying Mrs. Hirasawa's store. Although my husband did not have a steady job, I worked hard to support us, even attending beauty college to get licensed. After the war, I returned to Sacramento and bought back my beauty shop, though it was in poor condition.

After the war, I returned to that city and bought back my beauty shop, though it was in poor condition.

explicit_text: During the time of the Exposition, we made some money and decided to sell our store in San Francisco. This event helped us financially to move to Sacramento where I started a beauty shop. The Exposition was a turning point for our family's business decisions.

explicit_text: I learned the Marinaro system of beauty treatment in San Francisco at a beauty school. This system used electricity for hair waving, which was new to me. I was proud to be the only Japanese among about 300 people who took the manicure test and passed with a high score.

explicit_text: A man in the insurance business from Salt Lake City recommended that my husband sell insurance since he was not working. However, my husband did not do well, so I ended up selling insurance myself, often taking notes while he talked. Salt Lake City was indirectly connected to our efforts to find work.

explicit_text: Rev. Inori was a seminary student staying at the church when I was sick with a fever after arriving in America. He knew my older brother from Tokyo and kindly cooked breakfast for me. He also called my husband in Sacramento when I had to be hospitalized, showing his care and connection to my family.

explicit_text: Sacramento was where I opened my beauty shop and lived for many years. After the war, I returned there and bought back my shop, though it was in poor condition. The city was central to my efforts to support my family and rebuild after the war.

explicit_text: My husband was helping Dr. Kagawa in Japan during the war. I received a letter from him telling me to go back to Japan, but I did not have time to sell the store or catch the last ship. Dr. Kagawa's work was part of my husband's activities in Japan while I stayed in the U.S.

explicit_text: A pastor in Marysville helped my son Sammy get into the Air Force despite restrictions on Japanese Americans. Sammy stayed in Marysville at one point and met me at the station when I returned from Chicago after the war. Marysville was important in supporting my son's military career.

explicit_text: My son Sammy was stationed at Travis Air Force Base after basic training. He was allowed to come home on weekends when I injured my foot. Travis Air Force Base was where he worked as an assistant to an instructor during the war.

explicit_text: When the war broke out, I had to close my beauty shop and go to a relocation camp. I stored my equipment in government storage before leaving. Although I felt pitiful, I accepted the situation since everyone had to bear it. I taught Sunday School there and worked as a nurse's aide, and we were treated well with enough food and medicine.
